decision: f32 microkernel shape: 8x16 vs 6x16, decided at n=512
metric: best-min floor GFLOP/s over repeated cooldown-separated single-thread runs (noise-robust; a persistent background antivirus held CV above the 5% protocol gate, so the spike-resistant floor decides, not the median)
measurement_mode: single-thread (OMP_NUM_THREADS=1) at n=512 f32; the shape decision is a per-core register/port comparison, and the single-thread floor has CV ~10-26% vs the 55-155% the 6-thread region shows under the same AV load, so the per-core signal is the trustworthy one for this relative choice
rationale: The register-budget argument predicted 6x16 would win (8x16 needs 16 ymm accumulators, the whole file, forcing B reloads each k-step; 6x16 mirrors the f64 6x8 12-accumulator budget with 2 ymm free for B and 1 for the broadcast). Measurement overruled it: 8x16's higher arithmetic density (16 FMAs per k-step) amortizes the B reloads and won by ~8% on this Comet Lake core's port layout in two independent rounds. This is the case where f32 shape must be decided by measuring, not by counting registers.
caveats: Sub-protocol for absolute throughput: the 5% CV gate is not met on this machine because of an uncloseable background antivirus (the user's security tool). The numbers are quotable only as a relative shape comparison, not as an absolute f32 GFLOP/s claim.
Re-measure on a quiet machine before treating any single number here as protocol-grade.

Multi-threaded f32 at these blocking constants ({96,320,2048}, tuned for f64) anti-scales badly under the AV barrier preemption (6-thread floor ~14 GF/s vs single-thread ~55 GF/s at n=512); that is a threading/noise interaction, not a kernel-shape signal, which is why the decision used the single-thread floor.

manifest.processor: Intel64 Family 6 Model 165 Stepping 2, GenuineIntel (i7-10750H, Comet Lake, 6C/12T, AVX2+FMA)
